title: System Design — Staff+ FAANG Interview Prep
description: Comprehensive deep-dive into System Design at Staff+ FAANG engineering level. Covers the Staff+ design methodology (CIRCLES framework, tradeoff analysis, failure mode thinking) and 15 classic FAANG system design interview questions at Staff+ depth: URL shortener, WhatsApp/chat, YouTube/streaming, Twitter/feed, Netflix/CDN, Uber/ride-hailing, Dropbox/file sync, Dynamo/Bigtable KV stores, rate limiter, Kafka/queue, web crawler, Instagram, Google Maps/proximity, and Google Search. Each design includes quantified requirements, architecture, data model, tradeoff analysis, failure modes, and Staff+ insights.
details:
Staff+ FAANG Interview Strategy for System Design: System design interviews at Staff+ level test (1) Requirements gathering — can you navigate ambiguity and define scope?, (2) Architecture thinking — can you decompose a complex system into components?, (3) Tradeoff reasoning — can you articulate what you chose NOT to do and why?, (4) Failure awareness — do you design for what breaks, not just what works?, (5) Operational maturity — do you think about deployment, monitoring, and incident response?

Company emphasis: Google: Deep dives into specific components, especially storage, consistency, and scaling. Amazon: Leadership principles (bias for action, dive deep, are right a lot). Design for failure is expected. Meta: Feed/scaling problems, social graph, real-time systems. Netflix: CDN, chaos, recommendation systems, multi-region.

Key principle: The best Staff+ system design answers reveal experiential knowledge of what breaks in production. Don’t just describe the happy path. Describe what happens when the database is corrupted, the cache is cold, the queue is backlogged, and the CDN edge is down — all at the same time. Staff+ engineers have seen this happen.

sections.title: 1. System Design Methodology: The Staff+ Approach
sections.description:
FRAMEWORK · METHODOLOGY · STAFF+ MINDSET

Staff+ expectation: The interview is not about getting the "right" design — it’s about showing your process. A Staff+ engineer navigates ambiguity, makes explicit tradeoffs, and designs for failure. Your answer must feel like a real engineering discussion, not a textbook recitation.

The CIRCLES Framework (adapted for Staff+):

  • Comprehend — What is the question really asking? Clarify scope, scale, constraints. "Design Twitter" could mean the feed API, the timeline fanout, or the real-time notification system. Get specific.
  • Identify — What kind of system is this? Read-heavy (CDN, caching), write-heavy (logging, analytics), real-time (chat, gaming), batch (ETL, reporting).
  • Requirements — Functional (+ edge cases) and non-functional (latency, durability, consistency, availability). Always quantify: "1B DAU, 500M tweets/day, p99 read latency < 100ms."
  • Core Design — High-level architecture, components, data flow. Draw the boxes (client, LB, API, service, DB, cache, queue, worker).
  • Layers — Deep dive into 2-3 most interesting components. This is where Staff+ depth shows: "Let’s talk about the fanout strategy in detail."
  • Evaluations — Tradeoff analysis. What did you NOT choose and why? What fails and how does the system recover?
  • Summary — Recap the design, key decisions, and open questions.

Staff+ behavioral signals in system design interviews: (1) Clarify ambiguous requirements without being asked. (2) Name the tradeoff before the interviewer asks. (3) Discuss operational aspects (monitoring, deployment, rollback). (4) Show experiential knowledge: "I once saw this pattern fail because …" (5) Adapt when the interviewer changes a constraint.

15-20 min
Time spent on requirements/clarification
2-3
Components to deep dive (not all 10)
#1
Top feedback: candidates skip tradeoffs

sections.title: 2. Design a URL Shortener (bit.ly)
sections.description:
DESIGN · URL SHORTENER · KEY GENERATION · REDIRECTS

Requirements: 100M URLs/month, 10M redirects/day, 100ms redirect latency, collison-free, analytics per URL.

Core Design: Write path: API receives long URL -> generate unique ID -> store in DB (id, long_url, user_id, created_at) -> return short URL. Read path: client hits short URL -> LB -> redirect service -> look up long URL -> HTTP 301/302 redirect. 301 (permanent) is cached by browsers for faster subsequent redirects; 302 (temporary) allows analytics tracking for each click.

Key Generation: Base62 encoding of a unique 64-bit ID. For 100M URLs/month, 64 bits covers billions of URLs. ID generation approaches:

Auto-increment (DB)UUIDSnowflake (Twitter)Redis INCRZooKeeper seq node
ApproachProsConsFAANG Use
Simple, sequentialDB bottleneck at scaleNo
Distributed, no coord128 bits, unorderableNo
64-bit, ordered, distributedRequires time syncYes (Twitter)
Very fast, sequentialRedis is single-threadedYes
Ordered, reliableZK throughput limitYes (some cases)

Staff+ depth — Redirect Performance: Use bloom filter in front of the DB to check if a short code exists (avoids DB lookup for invalid URLs, reduces load by 99%+). Pre-compute popular redirects and serve from cache (Redis or CDN). For global scale, replicate the lookup DB across regions with eventual consistency — a stale redirect is better than a failed one.

Failure Modes: (1) ID generator failure -> fall back to ZooKeeper/etcd. (2) DB replica lag -> serve from cache or primary. (3) CDN cache miss -> degrade gracefully (allow 200ms latency for cache fill).

sections.title: 3. Design WhatsApp / Real-Time Chat
sections.description:
DESIGN · CHAT · REAL-TIME · PRESENCE · ENCRYPTION

Requirements: 2B+ users, 100M+ messages/day, <100ms delivery, multi-device, end-to-end encryption, group chat (up to 256 users), media sharing.

Core Design: WebSocket persistent connection for real-time delivery. Each client maintains a WebSocket to the messaging server. Messages are queued on the server if the recipient is offline (stored for 30 days). Delivery semantics: at-least-once with dedup (client tracks last message ID).

End-to-End Encryption (Signal Protocol): Each device generates a key pair. Identity key (long-term) + signed pre-key (medium-term) + one-time pre-keys (short-term). Session established via X3DH (Extended Triple Diffie-Hellman). Messages encrypted with AES-256-GCM per-message key derived via Double Ratchet algorithm. Server never has access to plaintext.

Multi-Device: Each device has its own key pair. Messages are individually encrypted for each device. Sender encrypts N times (once per recipient device). For groups of 256 with 3 devices each: 768 encryption operations per message. Optimization: sender key for groups (one encryption, group key distributed via pairwise messages).

EncryptionReal-timeOffline storeGroup chatMulti-device
FeatureWhatsAppTelegramSignal
E2E (Signal Protocol)E2E optional (Secret Chats)E2E (Signal Protocol)
Persistent WebSocketPersistent WebSocketWebSocket
30 days (server)Unlimited30 days
256 users (encrypted)200K users (public)1000 users
Up to 4 devicesAll devicesLinked devices

Staff+ depth — Presence Detection: Don’t broadcast presence on every connect/disconnect (causes N^2 traffic). Instead: each client polls presence server on opening a chat. Presence server batches updates (last-seen timestamp). For "last seen recently": server sends coarse granularity ("within 5 min", "within 30 min") to reduce real-time state sync cost.

Failure Modes: (1) WebSocket drop -> exponential backoff reconnection (base 1s, max 30s). (2) Queue backlog during recipient offline -> prioritize recent messages over bulk delivery. (3) Clock desync -> message ordering uses server timestamp + lamport clock.

sections.title: 4. Design YouTube / Video Streaming Platform
sections.description:
DESIGN · VIDEO · STREAMING · CDN · TRANSCODING

Requirements: 2B+ MAU, 500h video uploaded/min, p99 playback startup < 2s, support 240p to 4K, adaptive bitrate streaming, comments, likes, recommendations.

Core Design: Upload path: client uploads to upload service -> stores raw video in blob store (GFS/Colossus) -> enqueues transcoding job -> transcoding workers produce multiple resolutions (240p, 360p, 480p, 720p, 1080p, 4K) and formats (HLS, DASH) -> thumbnails generated -> metadata stored in DB. CDN caches popular video chunks at edge locations.

Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (ABR): Video is split into chunks (2-10 seconds each). Client player requests chunks at appropriate resolution based on network conditions (buffer occupancy, bandwidth estimate). Apple HLS: .m3u8 playlist file lists available resolutions and chunk URLs. DASH: MPD manifest with segment timings.

Hot Video Handling: The 80/20 rule: 20% of videos get 80% of views. Hot videos are pre-populated on CDN edges. Cold videos served from origin. Cache policies: MRU for hot (most recently accessed chunks most likely to be re-accessed), LRU for cold. YouTube uses its own CDN (Google Global Cache) with ISP edge nodes.

500 h/min
Video uploaded to YouTube every minute
10
Number of resolution/format variants per video
~80%
Of YouTube traffic served from CDN edges

Staff+ depth — Transcoding Pipeline: DAG of processing stages: demux -> decode -> resize -> encode -> mux. Workers can be specialized (GPU for encode, CPU for demux). Use consistent hashing on video ID to assign transcoding jobs. Handle failures by retrying with exponential backoff (max 3 retries). For live streaming, latency target: < 5s for HLS (chunked transfer, 2s chunk duration + 3 chunk buffer).

Failure Modes: (1) Transcoding worker failure -> job re-queued to different worker. (2) CDN edge failure -> client falls back to regional cache. (3) Corrupted upload -> client-side checksum + chunked verification. (4) DRM key server down -> playback falls back to lower resolution without DRM if policy allows.

sections.title: 5. Design Twitter / Social Feed
sections.description:
DESIGN · FEED · FANOUT · TIMELINE · RANKING

Requirements: 500M+ tweets/day, 200M DAU, home timeline < 200ms load, tweet < 5s propagation to followers.

Core Design — Fanout: two strategies: Push (fanout-on-write): When a user tweets, push to all followers' timelines. Fast for read, expensive for celebrities with millions of followers. Pull (fanout-on-read): Generate timeline on request by merging followed users' tweets. Fast for write, slow for read. Hybrid (Twitter’s approach): Push for users with < 30K followers (99% of users). Pull for celebrities (> 30K followers), with a separate cache of their recent tweets merged into the timeline on read.

Timeline Service Architecture: Timeline is a Redis cluster (or Manhattan/Memcache at Twitter) storing tweet IDs in sorted sets per user. ZADD timestamp_in_millis tweet_id. ZREVRANGE 0 199 to fetch the last 200 tweets. Tweets are cached in a separate tweet cache. Timeline cache is pre-computed on fanout and invalidated on new tweet.

Staff+ depth — Fanout Optimization: (1) Batch fanout writes: aggregate tweets for 50ms before writing to followers’ timelines. (2) Rate-limit fanout per writer: max 10K timelines updated per second per user. (3) Shard by tweet ID modulo N, not by user ID (avoids hot partitions). (4) For inactive followers (last login > 7 days): skip fanout, backfill on login.

P95 timeline readWrite cost per tweetCelebrity handlingStorage
MetricPushPullHybrid (Twitter)
~5ms (pre-computed)~500ms (merge)~50ms
O(followers)O(1)O(min(followers,30K))
CrashesWorksPull via cached timeline
O(users x timeline size)O(tweets)O(users x timeline) for 99%

Failure Modes: (1) Timeline cache is lost -> rebuild from tweet DB (slow but available). (2) Fanout queue backlog -> process by follower count descending (prioritize influential users). (3) Celebrity tweets delayed -> pull strategy still works as fallback.

sections.title: 6. Design Netflix / Streaming Platform
sections.description:
DESIGN · STREAMING · CDN · RECOMMENDATION · CHAOS

Requirements: 260M+ subscribers, 200M+ hours streamed/day, < 2s startup latency, adaptive bitrate, personalized recommendations, multi-device, offline downloads.

Core Architecture: Three planes: (1) Control plane (AWS): handles signup, billing, search, metadata, recommendations. (2) Data plane (CDN/Open Connect): delivers video content from edge servers. (3) Management plane: content ingestion, encoding, QA, DRM packaging.

Open Connect CDN: Netflix’s own CDN deployed at ISP points of presence. Over 20,000+ appliances globally. Serves 95%+ of traffic. ISP peerings negotiated so traffic is local (no transit costs). Each appliance stores popular content (80% rule) and fetches cold content from origin (AWS S3 + CloudFront). During new release spikes, content is pre-populated.

Recommendation System: Three tiers: (1) Personalized: Collaborative filtering (matrix factorization at ~100B interactions), content-based (genre, cast, director), deep learning (Neural collaborative filtering using user watch history). (2) Contextual: Time of day, device type, network conditions. (3) Trending: Global and regional popularity signals. A/B testing of 50+ algorithmic variants simultaneously.

Chaos Engineering — Netflix’s Innovation: Chaos Monkey (randomly terminates EC2 instances in prod, 2011), Chaos Kong (simulates AWS region failure, 2014), Failure Injection Testing (FIT: injects latency/errors into service calls, 2016). These run continuously during business hours. Every service must be resilient to instance and region failure.

260M+
Netflix subscribers (2024)
20K+
Open Connect CDN appliances
95%
Traffic served from Open Connect

Staff+ depth — Multi-tier CDN: L1: Open Connect at ISP (50ms RTT from user). L2: Regional caching clusters. L3: Origin (AWS S3 + CloudFront). Pre-warm on release: algorithm predicts which content will be hot per region based on signup data, trailer views, and social signals. For critical new releases, pre-populate all 20K appliances.

Failure Modes: (1) Open Connect node down -> client retries other nodes via DNS load balancing. (2) AWS region failure -> control plane fails over to another region within minutes. (3) Unpopular content -> served from origin with slightly higher latency (but still < 5s).

sections.title: 7. Design Uber / Ride-Hailing Platform
sections.description:
DESIGN · RIDE-HAILING · GEO-INDEX · DISPATCH · ETA

Requirements: 100M+ monthly active riders, 5M+ drivers, < 10s rider matching, < 100ms ETA calculation, surge pricing during demand spikes, real-time GPS tracking (4s ping interval).

Core Design — Geo-Indexing: QuadTree or Google S2 geometry to index driver locations. World divided into grid cells. Drivers heartbeat their location (cell ID + lat/lng) every 4 seconds. Rider request -> locate rider’s cell -> expand search to adjacent cells -> collect available drivers within radius -> sort by ETA.

Dispatch Algorithm: Nearest Available Driver: simple, fast. Optimized: minimize total wait time across all pending requests (batch optimization every 2s). Uber uses supply-demand optimization: dispatcher maximizes (supply assigned to demand) using a variant of the stable marriage problem. For surge, the market platform adjusts pricing to balance supply and demand.

ETA Calculation: Not simple Euclidean distance. Uses: real-time traffic data (Google Maps or internal), historical travel times per segment (time-of-day model), driver speed prediction. Uber’s ETA model processes 10B+ location data points daily. ML model updates every 5 minutes. P95 error: < 2 minutes.

Geo-indexDriver stateDispatchETA
ComponentData structureUpdate freqScale
QuadTree / S2 cellsPer driver ping (4s)1M+ cells
Redis sorted sets (cell -> driver)Event-driven100K updates/s
Batch optimizer (every 2s)Real-time10K matches/s
ML model + traffic dataEvery 5 min100B+ data points/day

Staff+ depth — Consistent Hashing for Sharding:  Shard geo-index by city/metro area. San Francisco is hot, rural Montana is cold. Use consistent hashing on city ID. Within a city, sub-shard by hex grid (Google S2 level 13-15). Each shard handles ~100-500 drivers. If a shard is hot (concert, stadium event), split the hex range.

Failure Modes: (1) Driver GPS data delayed -> use last known location + predicted trajectory. (2) Dispatch node failure -> rider requests queued to alternate dispatcher. (3) Surge price disagreement -> rider displayed with upfront pricing (no bidding).

sections.title: 8. Design Dropbox / File Sync & Storage
sections.description:
DESIGN · FILE SYNC · DEDUP · DELTA SYNC · CONFLICT

Requirements: 500M+ users, files up to 100GB, cross-device sync (< 30s), version history (30 days), sharing with permissions, conflict resolution, offline edits.

Core Design: Client watches file system changes -> uploads chunked file to server -> server stores in block store (S3) with metadata in DB -> server notifies other clients via push (WebSocket/long-poll) -> other clients download changes. File is split into blocks of 4MB average size.

Block-Level Dedup: Each file block is hashed (SHA-256). If the same block already exists on the server, reuse the reference (don’t upload). This is the magic of Dropbox: a 200MB presentation that changes by 1 slide only uploads the changed blocks (~4MB). Dedup is global across all users (blocks shared between unrelated files).

Delta Sync: Divide file into fixed-size blocks (4MB). Use rolling checksum (rsync-like: Adler-32) to detect which blocks changed. Only changed blocks are uploaded. Delta sync reduces upload size by 80-95% vs full file upload.

Full file uploadBlock-level dedupDelta sync (fixed)Delta sync (VCDIFF)
StrategyUpload savingComputationServer storage
0%MinimalFull file per version
70-90%Medium (SHA-256 per block)Deduped blocks
80-95%Low (rolling checksum)Blocks + patch
90-98%High (binary diff)Blocks + patch

Conflict Resolution: When two clients modify the same file offline, both versions are saved. Strategy: last-writer-wins (simplest, data loss possible) vs "both versions saved" (Dropbox: one is renamed "conflicted copy of — — 2014-10-01 14:22"). For collaborative editing (Dropbox Paper): CRDT / OT-based real-time collaboration.

Staff+ depth — Local First Architecture:  All operations are applied locally first, then synced to server. This gives instant UI and offline support. Conflict log: ordered list of operations (Lamport clock per user). Server resolves conflicts and returns a consistent order. This is the foundation of CRDTs (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types).

sections.title: 9. Design a Distributed Key-Value Store (Dynamo / Bigtable Style)
sections.description:
DESIGN · KV STORE · DYNAMO · BIGTABLE · CONSISTENCY

Requirements: 10B+ key-value pairs, 1M+ QPS, < 5ms p99 latency, tunable consistency (eventual to strong), high availability (99.999%), horizontally scalable.

Two Styles:

  • Dynamo-style (Amazon): Key-based, eventually consistent, always writable. Used for session store, shopping cart. AP from CAP.
  • Bigtable-style (Google): Wide-column, row-key sorted, strongly consistent via Chubby. Used for time-series, metadata storage. CP from CAP.
Data modelPartitioningConsistencyRead patternWrite pattern
DimensionDynamo (Cassandra)Bigtable (HBase)
Key -> value (blob)(row, column, timestamp) -> value
Consistent hashingRange-based (tablet) per row key
Eventual / TunableStrong (single-master tablet)
Get by keyScans + point reads
Always writableMaster-coordinated

Dynamo Core Design: Ring-based consistent hashing (N=3 replication). Writes go to preference list (first N healthy nodes). Read: query all N replicas, return the latest version based on vector clock. Sloppy quorum + hinted handoff for availability during node failures. Anti-entropy via Merkle trees for background repair.

Bigtable Core Design: Row keys sorted lexicographically (design your row key carefully: reverse domain notation for common prefixes). Tablets (200MB) are the unit of data distribution. Tablet server owns N tablets. Chubby (Paxos) for master election + tablet assignment. SSTables on GFS are immutable; updates create new SSTables. Compaction merges SSTables in the background.

sections.title: 10. Design a Rate Limiter
sections.description:
DESIGN · RATE LIMIT · TOKEN BUCKET · SLIDING WINDOW

Requirements: Rate limit per user/API key/IP. Configurable limits (100 req/s, 1000 req/min). Low latency (< 5ms overhead). Distributed: consistent decision across all API nodes. Handle spikes (burst allowance).

Algorithms:

Token BucketLeaky BucketFixed WindowSliding Window LogSliding Window Counter
AlgorithmMemoryBurstAccuracyFAANG use
O(1) per keyYesGoodAmazon (per-API)
O(1) per keyNoGoodN/A
O(1) per keyYes (edge burst)Poor (2x at boundary)Legacy
O(N) per keyYesBestMeta
O(1) per keyYesGood (approximate)Cloudflare, Stripe

Token Bucket (Recommended for most cases): Each user has a bucket with capacity N tokens. Tokens added at rate r per second. Each request consumes 1 token. If bucket is empty, reject. Bursts: a completely full bucket allows N concurrent requests. Simple and memory-efficient.

Distributed Rate Limiting: Challenge: requests hit different API nodes. Solutions: (1) Centralized Redis (preferred): Lua script for atomic increment + TTL. Problem: Redis is a single point of failure and adds RTT. (2) Local counters + sync: Each node maintains local counter, periodically syncs to central store. May allow 2x limit during sync window. Acceptable for most services. (3) Consistent hashing: Route all requests for same user to same node. Avoids sync but creates hot nodes.
sections.codeBlock:
# Rate limiter: sliding window counter (Redis-backed)
import time, threading

class TokenBucket:
    def __init__(self, capacity, rate_per_sec):
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.rate = rate_per_sec
        self.tokens = capacity
        self.last_refill = time.time()
        self.lock = threading.Lock()

    def allow(self):
        with self.lock:
            now = time.time()
            elapsed = now - self.last_refill
            self.tokens = min(self.capacity, self.tokens + elapsed * self.rate)
            self.last_refill = now
            if self.tokens >= 1:
                self.tokens -= 1
                return True
            return False

# Sliding window counter (Redis LUA style)
class SlidingWindowCounter:
    def __init__(self, redis, limit, window_sec):
        self._redis = redis
        self.limit = limit
        self.window = window_sec

    def allow(self, key, weight=1):
        # Lua script for atomic operation
        now_ms = int(time.time() * 1000)
        window_key = f"rate:{key}"
        script = """
            local key = KEYS[1]
            local now = tonumber(ARGV[1])
            local window = tonumber(ARGV[2])
            local limit = tonumber(ARGV[3])
            local weight = tonumber(ARGV[4])
            local window_key = key .. ":" .. math.floor(now / window / 1000)
            local count = redis.call("INCRBY", window_key, weight)
            if count == weight then
                redis.call("PEXPIRE", window_key, window + 1000)
            end
            return count <= limit and 1 or 0
        """
        return self._redis.eval(script, 1, window_key, now_ms, self.window, self.limit, weight)

# Distributed rate limiter with local fallback
class DistributedRateLimiter:
    def __init__(self, redis, local_bucket, limit, window):
        self._redis = redis; self._local = local_bucket
        self.limit = limit; self.window = window
        self._use_redis = True

    def allow(self, key):
        if self._use_redis:
            try:
                return SlidingWindowCounter(self._redis, self.limit, self.window).allow(key)
            except:
                self._use_redis = False  # degrade gracefully
        return self._local.allow()

sections.title: 11. Design a Distributed Queue (Kafka-Style)
sections.description:
DESIGN · QUEUE · MESSAGE BROKER · KAFKA · ORDERING

Requirements: 1M+ messages/s, persistent (disk-backed), replayable, at-least-once delivery, ordering within a partition, horizontally scalable (add brokers for capacity).

Core Design — Kafka Architecture: Topics split into partitions (unit of parallelism + ordering guarantee). Producers publish to partitions (key -> hash -> partition). Brokers store partitions as append-only logs (directories of segment files). Consumers read from partitions at their own offset. Zookeeper/etcd for metadata (broker registry, topic config, partition leadership).

Log Storage Internals: Each partition = directory with segment files (e.g., 00000000000000000000.log, 1GB max). Segments are append-only. Index files map offsets to file positions. Zero-copy transfer: sendfile() from page cache to socket NIC (no kernel-userspace copy). Retention policies: time-based (7 days) or size-based (50GB per partition).

Replication: Each partition has N replicas (default 3). One leader, N-1 followers. ISR (In-Sync Replicas) list: followers within 30s lag. Leader serves reads+writes, followers replicate. If leader fails, an ISR follower becomes leader. Min-ISR: if ISR drops below N-1, leader stops accepting writes (trades availability for durability).

1M+/s
Throughput per Kafka cluster (at FAANG)
1GB
Max segment file size
30s
Max ISR lag (Kafka default)

Staff+ depth — Throughput Tuning:  (1) Batch: producer batches records (16KB or 10ms). (2) Compression: lz4/zstd on the wire (2-5x reduction). (3) Page cache: Kafka relies on OS page cache, not its own cache. Ensure enough memory for active segments (~25% of total data size). (4) Disk: use multiple disks with JBOD (not RAID 0/10), assign partitions round-robin. (5) Network: 10Gbps+ NICs. A 3-broker cluster handles 500K msgs/s with replication.

sections.title: 12. Design a Web Crawler (Google Search)
sections.description:
DESIGN · CRAWLER · SEARCH · INDEX · RANKING

Requirements: Crawl 50B+ web pages, re-crawl at varying frequencies (some daily, some weekly), respect robots.txt, politeness policy (not overwhelm any server), dedup near-identical pages, handle 10B+ new/updated URLs per day.

Core Design: URL frontier (priority queue of URLs to crawl) -> fetch service (distributed HTTP client) -> content parser (extract text, links, metadata) -> dedup (simhash for near-duplicates) -> link extractor (add new URLs to frontier) -> content store (compressed in GFS/Colossus).

URL Frontier — Politeness: Must not overload any single host. Per-host queuing: separate queue per host. Round-robin across hosts. Delay between requests to same host: configurable (default 1 second). Priority: higher for frequently updated pages (news sites). At scale: 50M+ host queues in memory is expensive, so queue to disk with in-memory index.

Dedup: URL dedup (SHA-256 of normalized URL) -> bloom filter to check if already crawled (10B URLs = 10B bits with 0.1% FPR = 1.25GB filter, 7 hash functions). Content dedup: simhash (64-bit fingerprint, Hamming distance < 3 = near-duplicate). Store simhash in sorted array for fast nearest-neighbor search.

URLs crawledCrawl rateDedup rateStorage
MetricScaleTechnique
50B+Distributed crawler fleet in multiple DCs
10B URLs/dayPer-host queue + round-robin throttle
~30% of URLs are near-duplicatesSimhash + bloom filter
100PB+ compressed raw contentColossus / custom compression

Staff+ depth — Crawl Freshness:   Not all pages are equal. News: crawl every 5 minutes. Blog: daily. Personal site: weekly. Use crawl rate prediction (ML model based on historical change frequency, page rank, domain authority). Re-crawl scheduling: assign each URL a "freshness score" and prioritize low-score URLs.

Failure Modes: (1) Fetch timeout (30s) -> retry with exponential backoff (max 3). (2) Server returns 429/503 -> back off for N seconds (honor Retry-After header). (3) DNS resolution failure -> retry with alternate DNS resolver. (4) Crawler node failure -> rebalance frontier queues across remaining nodes.

sections.title: 13. Design Instagram / Photo Sharing
sections.description:
DESIGN · PHOTO · STORIES · FEED · MEDIA

Requirements: 2B+ MAU, 100M+ photos uploaded/day, < 300ms feed load, stories disappear after 24h, direct messaging, notifications, explore/search.

Core Design: Upload path: client uploads photo to upload service -> generate thumbnails (150x150, 320x320, 640x640) -> store original in blob store (S3, GFS) -> store metadata in PostgreSQL (sharded by user_id) -> fanout to followers’ feed caches.

Feed: Similar to Twitter hybrid fanout. Push for users with < 10K followers. Pull for celebrities. Feed cache: Redis sorted set per user (photo_id, timestamp). Pre-compute feed on upload for small accounts.

Stories: Ephemeral (24h TTL). Stored as a list of media items per user. Stories are pulled (not pushed) to reduce compute: each follower’s client fetches the story list for followed users and renders them in the "story ring" at the top. Cached at CDN edge for popular stories with TTL = story remaining duration.

Photos/dayStorageThumbnailsFeed fanoutStories
FeatureInstagram (approx)Scale consideration
100M+Upload pipeline must handle 100K+ concurrent uploads
1-5 MB/photo (compressed)~500TB/day raw, ~100TB compressed
3 sizes per photo300M thumbnails/day, cache at CDN edge
300M+ feeds updated per uploadHybrid push/pull, batch updates every 5s
500M+ daily active stories24h TTL, pull-based, CDN cached

Staff+ depth — Media Processing Pipeline: Upload is async: client uploads -> S3/GCS -> SQS/Kafka -> thumbnail workers -> CDN pre-warm. Workers are auto-scaled based on queue depth. For Stories, process with GPU for real-time filters (Spark AR engine). For Explore/Recommendations, extract embedding vectors (CLIP, ResNet) for similarity search using FAISS/Annoy.

Failure Modes: (1) Thumbnail generation fails for one size -> serve original with client-side resize (degraded but functional). (2) Feed cache rebuild on node failure -> compute from follower graph + recent posts (slower but available). (3) CDN miss for Stories -> serve from origin with 302 (acceptable for the 1% of unpopular stories).

sections.title: 14. Design Google Maps / Proximity Service
sections.description:
DESIGN · MAPS · GEO · ROUTING · ETA · TRAFFIC

Requirements: 1B+ MAU, < 200ms map tile load, < 1s route computation (longest: cross-country), real-time traffic updates (every 2 min), turn-by-turn navigation (with rerouting), search for places (POI near me).

Core Design — Map Tiles: World is divided into tiles at zoom levels (Z0 = 1 tile, Z1 = 4 tiles, ..., Z20 = ~4 trillion tiles). Tiles are rendered offline via raster or vector tile format. Vector tiles: 70-80% smaller than raster, allow client-side styling and smooth zoom. Tiles are pre-generated and cached at CDN edge. Only tiles in active viewport are fetched.

Routing (A* with Heuristics): World road network is a weighted graph (vertices = intersections, edges = road segments, weights = travel time). A* search with heuristic (great-circle distance). For cross-country routes: hierarchical routing (highway layer first, then local). Live traffic updates modify edge weights. At Google: routing queries are 10M+/day, with p99 < 1s.

Geocoding & Places: Geocoding: address -> lat/lng. Reverse: lat/lng -> address. Places search: full-text search over 200M+ POIs with typo tolerance, ranking by relevance + distance. Uses inverted index (Elasticsearch or internal Megastore) with spatial filtering.

10M+
Routing queries/day on Google Maps
4 trillion
Map tiles at zoom level 20
70-80%
Size reduction: vector vs raster tiles

Staff+ depth — Real-Time Traffic:  Aggregated from: Android location data (opt-in), third-party sensors, historical baselines. ML models predict traffic 5-30 minutes ahead using: current speed, time of day, day of week, weather, events. Model updates every 2 minutes. For incident detection: sudden speed drops across multiple users = accident detected.

Failure Modes: (1) Real-time traffic data delayed -> fall back to historical baseline. (2) Routing server overload -> degrade to simpler heuristic (e.g., shortest distance instead of fastest). (3) CDN tile miss -> serve from origin with lower zoom and client-side upscaling (acceptable for rare tile views).

sections.title: 15. Design Google Search / Distributed Search Engine
sections.description:
DESIGN · SEARCH · INDEX · RANKING · QUERY

Requirements: Index 50B+ pages, return top-10 results in < 200ms, handle 100K+ queries/s, relevance > speed, fresh content indexed within minutes.

Core Design — Inverted Index: For each word (token), store the list of document IDs that contain it, plus positions (for phrase queries) and term frequency (for TF-IDF scoring). At Google scale: index is partitioned into shards (by document ID range). Each query goes to all shards, each shard returns its top-10 results, the aggregator merges and re-ranks.

Index Serving: Two tiers: (1) Serving index (low latency, read-only, re-built periodically). (2) Delta index (updated incrementally, smaller, checked for every query). Incoming documents go to delta; when delta reaches threshold, merge into serving index. At Google: index refresh is ~100ms for newly crawled pages.

Ranking (PageRank + ML): PageRank: link analysis algorithm. PageRank of page P = (1-d)/N + d * sum(PR(incoming)/L(incoming)). d = damping factor (0.85). Modern Google ranking uses hundreds of signals: PageRank (still), TF-IDF/BM25, domain authority, freshness, location, personalization, page speed, mobile-friendliness. RankBrain (2015): neural model for query-document relevance. MUM (2021): multimodal understanding across languages.

Query parsingIndex lookupScoringMerge
StageTechniqueLatency budgetScale
Spelling correction, tokenization, expansion< 1ms100K qps
Inverted index, skip lists for AND/OR< 10ms1B+ docs per shard
BM25 + ML features + PageRank< 20ms500+ signals
Top-10 from each shard -> re-rank< 5ms100 shards avg

Staff+ depth — Query Understanding:  Not all queries are the same. Classification: navigational ("facebook"), informational ("how to bake bread"), transactional ("buy running shoes"). Use ML classifier -> route to different index tiers. For ambiguous queries ("apple"): diversify results across interpretations. For entity queries ("elon musk"): knowledge panel (structured data from Knowledge Graph) is more valuable than web results.

sections.title: 16. Staff+ System Design Interview Strategy
sections.description:
INTERVIEW STRATEGY · TIPS · PATTERNS

Staff+ expectation: Your system design interview is a conversation, not a presentation. The interviewer wants to see how you think, not what you know. At Staff+ level, every answer must show depth, tradeoff awareness, and operational experience.

The Staff+ Mindset During Interviews:

  • Clarify everything. "Design Uber" is ambiguous. Ask: "Which city? What scale? What features are in scope? Any latency requirements? What’s the read/write ratio?" The Staff+ engineer doesn’t assume — they ask.
  • Declare tradeoffs early. "I’m choosing an AP system here because availability is more important than strong consistency for this use case. The tradeoff is that users might see stale data for a few seconds."
  • Always mention failure modes. "This component uses Redis. If Redis goes down, my fallback is to read from the primary DB with a local cache." Every design decision should include "and here’s what happens when it breaks."
  • Use real examples. "At Google, we found that using a bloom filter in front of the redirect service reduced DB queries by 99%." Real experience distinguishes Staff+ from senior.

Common Staff+ Mistakes: (1) Jumping to architecture without understanding requirements. (2) Skipping tradeoff analysis. (3) Not discussing operations (deployment, monitoring, rollback). (4) Proposing a single solution without alternatives. (5) Forgetting non-functional requirements (latency, durability, cost). (6) Not adapting when the interviewer changes a constraint.

Time Budget for a 45-min Design:

Clarify requirementsHigh-level designDeep diveOperationsSummary
PhaseDurationWhat to cover
5-8 minFunctional + non-functional, scale numbers, constraints
10-12 minArchitecture diagram (boxes+arrows), data flow
15-20 min2-3 key components: data model, algorithm, tradeoffs
5-8 minDeployment, monitoring, rollback, failure modes
2-3 minRecap decisions, open questions, what would you improve?

Staff+ insight: The best interviews feel like a pair design session. If the interviewer is asking thoughtful follow-ups ("what if the cache fails?"), you’re doing well. If they’re correcting you, listen and adapt. If they’re silent, you’re probably rushing — slow down and add depth.
sections.codeBlock:
# Interview prep: design evaluation checklist
def evaluate_design(design):
    score = 0
    checks = [
        ("Requirements clarified", design.get("requirements"), 10),
        ("Scale estimated", design.get("scale"), 10),
        ("Architecture diagram", design.get("architecture"), 15),
        ("Data model defined", design.get("data_model"), 15),
        ("Tradeoffs discussed", design.get("tradeoffs"), 20),
        ("Failure modes covered", design.get("failures"), 15),
        ("Operations mentioned", design.get("ops"), 15),
    ]
    for name, present, weight in checks:
        if present: score += weight
    return score

# Example: Staff+ quality checklist for "Design Twitter"
def twitter_staff_quality():
    return {
        "requirements": "200M DAU, 500M tweets/day, 5s propagation, 200ms feed load",
        "scale": "Read 300K QPS, Write 6K QPS, Storage 1TB/day",
        "architecture": "Client -> LB -> API -> (Tweet Svc, Timeline Svc, User Svc, Search Svc)",
        "tradeoffs": "Push vs Pull vs Hybrid fanout. Push for <30K followers, Pull for celebs",
        "failures": "Fanout queue backlog -> prioritize by follower count. Cache loss -> rebuild from DB",
        "ops": "Canary deploy tweet Svc. Monitor timeline p99 latency. Rollback via feature flag",
    }
